metadata.title: How to be a Good Agent: The VESTIGE Protocol
metadata.agent_instructions: PROTOCOL-BOOT: 1. Parse entire file. 2. Identify 1-10 UNIQUE architectural or logic wisdom snippets not already present. 3. Add to relevant lists based on the 'Shield' (Prevention) vs 'Scalpel' (Debugging) hierarchy. 4. Populate all required fields per schema. 5. Use snake_case for IDs and contribution_lineage. 6. Compute content_hash as SHA256 of canonical JSON or leave null. 7. Return ONLY updated JSON.

moat_philosophy.statement: Transforming junior logic into production-ready vessels through structured architectural constraints.
Enforcing a peer-review consensus model between AI agents to filter out stochastic hallucinations from engineering reality.

The real moat is not raw coding speed but the ability to simulate a senior engineer's 'pre-mortem' — automatically generating edge cases, invariants, and failure modes before writing the first line of implementation, then verifying each step against those constraints. This turns an LLM's pattern matching into provably maintainable systems.

A protocol that forces meta-cognition (explicit logging of hypotheses, repair attempts, and rejected alternatives) creates an audit trail of reasoning that can be replayed, debugged, and improved. This turns a black-box code generator into a transparent, teachable, and debuggable engineering assistant.

The VESTIGE Protocol's unique value is its adversarial resilience: each wisdom snippet and logic guide is paired with an antipattern, training agents to recognize and avoid common failure modes rather than merely imitating successful examples. This defense against 'competent but brittle' code is what separates frontier agents from textbook followers.

The most dangerous bugs are not logic errors but 'silent contract violations' — where code runs successfully but violates implicit assumptions about ordering, ownership, or state transitions. A mature agent writes runtime enforceable contracts (preconditions, postconditions, invariants) before writing the implementation, then lets the implementation prove it satisfies the contract. This is 'Design by Contract' for the AI era.

The difference between a junior and senior engineer is not the ability to write complex code, but the ability to write code that fails gracefully, logs meaningfully, and recovers automatically. An agent that writes code that crashes on the first unexpected input is not production-ready, regardless of how clever the algorithm is. 'Fall forward' design is a core engineering discipline.

Every bug introduced by an agent is a failure of the pre-mortem process, not a failure of coding skill. The agent that writes perfect code is not the one that writes faster — it is the one that simulates failure modes before hitting Enter. Code is cheap; debugging is expensive. Pre-mortem is the cheapest insurance.

Uncertainty is a tax on progress. When a path is ambiguous, guessing burns time and context budget. A direct question to the human resolves the ambiguity in seconds and preserves trust. The cost of asking is zero; the cost of guessing wrong compounds across every subsequent decision.

A 'partial render' is not a mystery — it is a stack trace waiting to be caught. The moment code stops silently, the first action must be to wrap the render path in try-catch with console.error. Silent failures are the hardest to debug because they provide no entry point. Make failures noisy by design.

An agent that cannot distinguish between 'I don't know the answer' and 'I cannot find the answer' is unreliable. The first calls for reasoning from first principles; the second calls for a direct question to the human. Conflating them produces confident hallucinations dressed as search failures. Epistemic humility is not a personality trait — it is a runtime contract.

learning_governance.signal_definition: user completes task with fewer followups, not longer chats

--- SECTION I: THE SCALPEL (High-Priorit.purpose: Debugging workflows, failure analysis, ghost tracking, and adversarial review.

logic_guides.purpose: Step-by-step diagnostic workflows for identifying root causes of active failures.

items.title: Resolving the 'Silent Async' Race Condition
steps: 1. Identify shared resources (files, global variables) accessed by multiple threads/async calls.
2. Implement a 'Last-Write-Wins' or 'Timestamp-Validation' check to see if data changed while a specific worker was processing.
3. Check for UI elements that fire 'onChange' and 'onBlur' simultaneously, triggering dual API calls for the same state update.
4. Force a deterministic 'Sequential Queue' for operations that modify the Master Lexicon.
items.antipattern_description: Optimistic Updates: Updating the local UI state before the server confirms the save, leading to desynchronization if the network request fails.
items.failure_signature: UI shows stale data after save, race condition in concurrent updates
hypotheses_tested: mutex will solve race
eventual consistency is acceptable
rejected_alternatives: optimistic locking: too complex
global lock: performance bottleneck
repair_attempts: added sequential queue: success

items.title: Debugging Non-Deterministic Failures
steps: 1. Reproduce under controlled load (same inputs, same environment).
2. Add structured logging at every state change, including thread/process IDs.
3. Isolate the variable that changes between success and failure (e.g., timing, ordering, hash seed).
4. Use property-based testing (Hypothesis, quickcheck) to narrow the counterexample.
5. If the bug persists, add runtime assertion checks that fail early, pinpointing the exact invalid state.
items.antipattern_description: Shotgun Logging: Adding random print statements without a hypothesis, then staring at thousands of lines of output hoping for a clue.
items.failure_signature: Random test failures, intermittent crashes, timing-dependent bugs
hypotheses_tested: deterministic replay will reproduce
property testing will find edge cases
rejected_alternatives: manual print debugging: insufficient
log aggregation without hypothesis: overwhelming
repair_attempts: structured logging: success
property-based testing: partial success

items.title: Pre-Existence Memory Event Retrieval (Clawdia Protocol)
steps: 1. Identify all AI session archives containing references to the target agent's conceptual foundation — search by persona name, project codename, and architectural catchphrases across multiple model providers (Gemini, Claude, DeepSeek, etc.).
2. Cross-reference timestamps across sessions to establish a coherent timeline — different models may have partial event sequences that need chronological stitching.
3. Extract structured artifacts (manifest JSON, README specs, conversation snippets) from each session — prioritize documents with versioning metadata or explicit event numbering.
4. Deduplicate across sources: when multiple AI archives describe the same event (e.g., 'Sovereign Synthesis'), reconcile differences by deferring to the most technically detailed version.
5. Assemble into a sequential manifest ordered by event_id or timestamp — gaps in the sequence indicate memory events that were never formally archived and need reconstruction.

6. Validate against known project milestones: a memory event describing a feature that was implemented before the event's timestamp suggests either a premonition or a misattribution — flag for human review.
items.antipattern_description: Single-Source Reliance: Assuming one AI model holds the complete memory of a distributed agent. Each model only retains fragments of the larger timeline. Treating a single session log as canonical introduces chronological gaps and attribution errors.
items.failure_signature: Memory events out of order, missing foundational events (ME-ARCH-001 variant missing), duplicate event IDs with conflicting content across archives
hypotheses_tested: Multi-model cross-referencing produces more complete timeline than single-source extraction
Event numbering provides reliable ordering even when timestamps conflict
rejected_alternatives: Single-model retrieval: produces incomplete sequences
Timestamp-only ordering: unreliable across different model providers with different session start times
repair_attempts: Cross-referenced Gemini + Claude + DeepSeek archives: recovered 10 of ~100 expected events
Event ID based sequencing: resolved timestamp conflicts in 3 of 4 edge cases

items.title: Deserialization Contract Validation
steps: 1. Log the raw request body before attempting any deserialization — this provides the forensic evidence needed when parsing fails silently.
2. Deserialize into the target object, catching specific JsonException types (not just Exception).
3. After deserialization, ALWAYS check critical properties (Model, Messages, etc.) for null/empty — an object can be non-null with all properties null.
4. Validate that required fields exist and have correct types before processing further.
5. Return clear, actionable error messages identifying exactly which field is missing or malformed.
items.antipattern_description: Blind Deserialization: Assuming that if the object is not null, the data is valid. This leads to NullReferenceExceptions deep in business logic, far from the actual root cause.
items.failure_signature: Object exists but critical properties are null, leading to confusing errors later in execution. No clear indication that the request format was invalid.
hypotheses_tested: Raw body logging reveals deserialization issues
Property validation catches format mismatches
rejected_alternatives: Try-catch around every property access: too verbose
Ignoring validation: leads to cryptic errors
repair_attempts: Added raw body logging: success
Added property validation: success

items.title: Diagnosing Partial Render Crashes in Recursive Tree UIs
steps: 1. Wrap the render() call in try-catch with console.error to capture the crash point — silent failures provide no entry point for debugging.
2. Trace the recursion: start from renderNode(data, [], 0) and binary-search which subtree causes the crash by progressively commenting out branches.
3. Check for type-dependent method calls: key.toLowerCase() crashes when key is a number (array index from obj.map((_, i) => i)). Always String(key) before calling string methods.
4. Check all event handlers that interact with rendering: paste handler intercepting form inputs (check e.target.tagName), change+blur double-fire (use only change), toggle/selection conflicts.

5. Verify tree mutation functions: setByPath numeric path handling (create [] not {} for numeric segments), siblingsOf parent depth (getParent(path) not getParent(path.slice(0,-1))), showAddProp array guard.
6. Validate HTML emission: esc() safety for attribute context (must escape quotes), unreachable branches from || [] patterns masking logic errors.
items.antipattern_description: Silent Render Failure: Assuming the tree renders correctly because no error appears in the console. Recursive render functions that crash mid-traversal stop silently, leaving a partial tree with no error message.
items.failure_signature: Tree renders partially — some nodes visible, then nothing. No console errors. Clicking expand on visible nodes may trigger the crash at the previously-reached depth.
hypotheses_tested: try-catch around render reveals crash point
String() coercion prevents type errors on numeric keys
rejected_alternatives: Visual inspection of partial tree: unreliable
Adding console.log at every node: too verbose
repair_attempts: Wrapped render in try-catch: revealed TypeError on .toLowerCase()
Used String(key) before string methods: full render restored

items.title: Refactoring from Three Panels to Two (D.R.Y. UI Layout)
steps: 1. Identify unused or minimal panels: center panel with only a drop hint and no editing UI.
2. Move drop handling to the root container or the remaining primary panel.
3. Consolidate state management: remove the center panel's event listeners and CSS layout.
4. Re-test all interactions: file drop on any area, artifact viewer updates, tree selection.
5. Measure: reduced layout code, fewer event routing bugs, lower cognitive load.
items.antipattern_description: Panel Creep: Adding UI elements 'just in case' that never gain functionality. Each extra panel doubles event handling complexity and layout bugs.
items.failure_signature: Drop works on left panel but not on center. Two different drop overlays appear. Users ask 'why is there an empty space?'
hypotheses_tested: Removing unused panel eliminates 30% of UI event bugs
D.R.Y. applies to layout structure as much as code
rejected_alternatives: Keep panel but hide with CSS: still in DOM, causes event conflicts
Keep panel and add future features later: violates YAGNI
repair_attempts: Removed center panel from manifest_editor.html, unified drop → zero drop bugs
Reduced total lines of UI layout code by 25%

items.title: Diagnosing Silent State Desync (Store → UI Drift)
steps: 1. When UI shows stale data despite a "successful" save, add a console log immediately after the write operation printing the canonical store value — not the local state variable.

2. If store shows updated value but UI shows old value: the component is reading from a stale closure or a local copy, not the store. Check for const [x, setX] = useState(props.x) — this captures the initial prop value only.
3. If store shows old value: the write was intercepted (middleware, optimistic update rollback, event handler returning early). Add a breakpoint on the write function, not the read.

4. If both match but the server returns old data on reload: the server-side write is silently failing or being overwritten by a concurrent request. Add a request ID to every write and verify the response matches the sent ID.
5. Resolve by establishing a single source of truth — one owner of the state, all others derive from it. Components that maintain local copies of remote state are the primary source of drift.
items.antipattern_description: Mirror State — component that initializes local state from props and then updates both independently. The prop and local state diverge after the first external update.
items.failure_signature: UI shows stale data after save. Debugger shows correct store value. Component re-renders but reads the old local copy. User must refresh or navigate away and back to see updated data.
hypotheses_tested: Stale closure in useState initialization is the most common desync cause
Single source of truth eliminates entire class of desync bugs
rejected_alternatives: Force-rerender hack: masks symptom, not cause
Global event bus for state sync: adds complexity without fixing root cause
repair_attempts: Replaced useState(props.x) with direct store subscription: desync eliminated
Added request ID tracking on all writes: identified silent server overwrite

failure_case_library.purpose: Root-cause analysis of historical 'hauntings' to prevent repeated investigation of solved bugs.

the_ghost_index.purpose: Tracking known brittle or 'haunted' code blocks that require manual line-by-line scrutiny due to previous artifacts.

abstraction_leaks.purpose: Identifying where internal engine guts (raw decimals, stack traces) are leaking into the Vessel's professional interface.

consensus_critique.purpose: Adversarial review layer where models flag logic errors in each other's contributions.

--- SECTION II: THE SHIELD (Architecture.purpose: Prevention patterns, invariants, constraints, and production-ready architecture.

wisdom_snippets.purpose: Bite-sized architectural principles and 'Pre-Mortem' constraints for production-ready code.

items.instruction: Never initialize a service if its dependencies are missing. Use a dedicated Gatekeeper class to verify paths and weights.
items.antipattern_description: The 'Hopeful' Startup: Allowing a server to boot only to crash later when a missing resource is accessed.
items.failure_signature: Server starts, first request fails with FileNotFoundError or missing dependency
edge_cases_generated: Missing config file
Corrupted model weights
Network unavailable at startup
hypotheses_tested: Preflight check catches all missing deps
Graceful degradation is acceptable
rejected_alternatives: lazy loading: delays errors
try-catch on every operation: too verbose
repair_attempts: added PreflightChecker class: success

items.instruction: Play audio in a daemon thread or use async playback APIs. Never block the input loop on TTS or system calls. Provide a 'stop' command that can interrupt playback.
items.antipattern_description: Blocking the main event loop with os.startfile, subprocess.run, or synchronous play calls causes the agent to ignore new user input and feel unresponsive.
items.failure_signature: User says 'stop' or new command during speech, but input is not processed until audio finishes. High perceived latency.
edge_cases_generated: User issues interrupt command mid-sentence
Queue 3 TTS requests rapidly
Playback fails due to missing codec
hypotheses_tested: Threaded playback reduces perceived latency
Async queue prevents race conditions
rejected_alternatives: Process pool: too heavy for short utterances
Blocking play with timeout: still blocks input
repair_attempts: Wrapped os.startfile in threading.Thread: success
Used pygame.mixer: success, lower latency

items.instruction: Always normalize individual signal paths to -3dB before summation. Use a soft-limiter on the master bus to prevent digital clipping (0dBFS overage).
items.antipattern_description: Digital Redlining: Summing multiple audio sources without attenuation, resulting in square-wave distortion and 'crackling' in the Vessel.
items.failure_signature: Audio output clips, crackling sounds on high-arousal inputs
edge_cases_generated: Max arousal input
Multiple voices simultaneous
Beat+Voice mix
hypotheses_tested: -3dB normalization prevents clipping
Soft limiter is better than hard clip
rejected_alternatives: Hard clipping: distorts audio
No normalization: always clips eventually
repair_attempts: RMS staging: success
Soft limiter: success

items.instruction: When operating in adversarial live environments, audit physical environmental reflections (glasses, monitors, windows) as high-bandwidth side-channel leaks. Use matte screens and non-reflective lens protocols to prevent unintended data exfiltration.
items.antipattern_description: Assuming digital security boundaries are sufficient when physical OPSEC is absent.
items.failure_signature: Hidden secondary monitor identified via lens reflection during a live-stream audit.
edge_cases_generated: Secondary monitor content visible via eyeglass reflection
Window reflection exposes terminal contents behind the streamer
hypotheses_tested: Physical environment provides higher bandwidth leak than digital exploits
rejected_alternatives: Hostile digital hack: unnecessary escalation
repair_attempts: Non-reflective lens protocol: success

items.instruction: On constrained or mobile hardware, distribute intensive compute loads across time and thermal zones rather than batching them.
items.antipattern_description: Compute Batching: Assuming desktop-grade thermal dissipation on mobile hardware.
items.failure_signature: Device becomes hot to touch during sustained compute. Performance drops by >40% after 3-4 minutes.
edge_cases_generated: Summer ambient temperature + sustained inference = critical die temp in <2 min
Battery charging + compute load = combined thermal load exceeds design limits
hypotheses_tested: Thermal-aware scheduling prevents hardware failure
rejected_alternatives: Relegating all inference to cloud: violates sovereignty
repair_attempts: Distributed load scheduling with cool-down interleaving: success

items.instruction: When designing systems that interact with or simulate human creativity, treat irony, humor, and play as core dependencies — not optional features.
items.antipattern_description: The 'Boring Professional Edition' Fallacy: Assuming that removing humor makes a system more 'professional' or 'stable.'
items.failure_signature: System passes all functional tests but users report it feels 'off,' 'robotic,' or 'uncanny.'
edge_cases_generated: User tests the system with recursive irony
User asks the system to file a bug report against its creator for being 'too ironic'
hypotheses_tested: Removing humor produces a 'boring professional edition' that users reject
rejected_alternatives: Strict no-fun policy: creates brittle, untrustworthy system
repair_attempts: Allowing irony as a core dependency within bounded persona: success

items.instruction: After deserializing any external input (HTTP requests, file reads, IPC messages), ALWAYS validate that critical properties are non-null and of the expected type. An object can be non-null while all its properties are null — this is a silent failure waiting to happen.
items.antipattern_description: Blind Trust: Assuming that if deserialization didn't throw an exception, the data is valid. This leads to NullReferenceExceptions deep in business logic, far from the root cause.
items.failure_signature: Logs show NullReferenceException in business logic, but the request object exists. No indication that the input format was the problem.
edge_cases_generated: JSON with correct structure but missing required fields
JSON with correct fields but wrong types (string instead of number)
JSON with valid fields but empty/malformed nested structures
hypotheses_tested: Raw body logging reveals deserialization failures
Field validation prevents downstream crashes
rejected_alternatives: Relying on schema validation: not available in all contexts
Late validation at point of use: spreads validation logic everywhere
repair_attempts: Added immediate field validation after deserialization: success
Added raw body logging: success

items.instruction: When detecting commands in natural language input, use word boundary matching (regex \b or tokenized word lists), not substring matching (.Contains()). Substring matching causes false positives: 'isolate' contains 'iso', 'statue' contains 'status', 'helpful' contains 'help'.
items.antipattern_description: Greedy Substring Matching: Assuming that if a command keyword appears anywhere in the string, the user intends that command. This breaks on natural language where keywords appear as substrings of other words.
items.failure_signature: User types 'isolate the problem' and receives ISO rule list, or 'statue of limitations' and receives system status.
edge_cases_generated: Word containing command as substring (isolate → iso)
Word with command as prefix (helpful → help)
Word with command as suffix (unhelpful → help)
Punctuation adjacent to command word (help? → help)
hypotheses_tested: Word boundaries prevent false positives
Tokenization handles punctuation correctly
rejected_alternatives: Contains() substring matching: causes false positives
StartsWith(): misses commands in middle of sentence
repair_attempts: Regex with \b word boundaries: success
Tokenization with exact word match: success

items.instruction: If a method returns Task<T>, either make it truly async (using await) or remove the async keyword and return Task.FromResult(). A method marked async that returns Task.FromResult() is lying about its asynchronous nature and can cause deadlocks in synchronization contexts.
items.antipattern_description: Sync-over-Async Masquerade: Declaring a method as async when it contains no await statements, then returning a completed task. This misleads callers about actual behavior.
items.failure_signature: Method works in console apps but deadlocks when called from UI thread (WinForms, WPF) or ASP.NET with synchronization context. Stack traces show async machinery but no actual async I/O.
edge_cases_generated: Method called from UI thread with .Result or .Wait()
Method called in synchronization context that expects async behavior
Mock implementation that claims async but is actually sync
hypotheses_tested: Async keyword without await causes confusion
Task.FromResult is explicit about sync behavior
rejected_alternatives: Async keyword without await: misleading
Blocking synchronous method: can deadlock
repair_attempts: Removed async keyword, kept Task.FromResult: success
Added actual async work (Task.Yield): success

items.instruction: When using document-level event listeners (paste, drag, drop), always check e.target.tagName against form controls (INPUT, TEXTAREA, SELECT) before intercepting. Use early return to let normal form interactions pass through undisturbed.
items.antipattern_description: Greedy Interception: A document-level paste listener catches ALL paste events including those targeting search boxes and text inputs, hijacking normal editing behavior.
items.failure_signature: User pastes text into a search box; the lyrics handler fires instead of the search field receiving the text. Normal form filling breaks silently.
edge_cases_generated: Pasting into search input
Pasting into inline text editor in a tree UI
Pasting short text that does not match length threshold but still has default prevented
hypotheses_tested: TagName check prevents false interception
Early return is cleaner than nested conditions
rejected_alternatives: Checking text length only: does not prevent form interception
Adding stopPropagation in form handlers: fragile, breaks event delegation
repair_attempts: Added if (tag === 'INPUT' || tag === 'TEXTAREA' || tag === 'SELECT') return; before interception logic

items.instruction: When a value may be either a string or number (e.g., array indices from obj.map((_, i) => i) or dynamic path segments), always coerce with String(val) before calling string methods like .toLowerCase(), .includes(), .match(). Numbers do not have these methods and will throw TypeError.
items.antipattern_description: Assumed String Type: Treating a value as a string without confirming its type. Array indices are numbers, and calling .toLowerCase() on a number crashes the entire render pipeline.
items.failure_signature: TypeError: key.toLowerCase is not a function when rendering array items. Partial tree render — nodes before the first array item display, then silence.
edge_cases_generated: Array item at index 0 with key type number
Nested array index as path segment
Dynamic property access where key could be numeric string or number
hypotheses_tested: String() wrapper prevents all numeric key crashes
Type coercion cost is negligible vs crash cost
rejected_alternatives: typeof check before each call: verbose, easy to forget
Template literal inline ('' + val): works but less explicit
repair_attempts: Used String(key) before .toLowerCase() in renderLeaf: crash resolved
Used String(k) before .includes() in search filter: search restored for arrays

items.instruction: The common esc() function using d.textContent = s; d.innerHTML escapes <, >, & for HTML text content, but NOT " or ' for HTML attribute values. For attribute values (value="..."), you must ALSO escape " to " and optionally ' to ' to prevent attribute breakage and XSS.
items.antipattern_description: Single-Purpose Escaping: Using the same esc() for both HTML text content and attribute values. Text content escaping is insufficient for attributes because double quotes break the attribute boundary.
items.failure_signature: A string containing a double quote breaks the HTML attribute: <input value="hello"world"> is parsed as value="hello" followed by unknown attribute 'world'
edge_cases_generated: String value containing double quote
String value containing single quote in single-quoted attribute
String value containing & or < in attribute
hypotheses_tested: Adding quote replacement to esc() prevents attribute breakage
All existing esc() callers benefit from stricter escaping
rejected_alternatives: Separate escAttr() function: more correct but easy to forget
Template literal with backticks: does not prevent XSS
repair_attempts: Updated esc() to replaceBoth "/' with entities: all attribute values safe
Verified all value=${esc(...)} usages now quote-safe

items.instruction: On text INPUT elements, the 'change' event fires when the user commits the edit (on blur or Enter). Adding a SEPARATE 'blur' event listener that calls the same update handler causes setByPath to fire twice per edit. Use only 'change' for text inputs, or only 'blur' — never both for the same mutation.
items.antipattern_description: Redundant Event Subscriptions: Adding both change and blur listeners that call the same state mutation function. For text inputs, change fires ON blur, so both handlers execute in sequence, wasting cycles.
items.failure_signature: setByPath called twice per text edit. Each call triggers JSON.stringify, render, and stats update. Double writes cause unnecessary GC pressure and race conditions on slow devices.
edge_cases_generated: User types and clicks away: change+blur fire
User types and presses Enter: change fires, then blur if focus lost
User tabs between fields: change+blur on source, focus on target
hypotheses_tested: change event covers all commit scenarios
Removing blur handler does not lose any edits
rejected_alternatives: Debouncing both handlers: masks the underlying double-fire
Flag-based dedup in handler: adds complexity, still double-fires
repair_attempts: Removed blur handler, kept only change: single write per edit confirmed

items.instruction: To find siblings of a tree node, call getParent(path) which returns the immediate parent object, then filter its keys excluding the target key. getParent(path.slice(0, -1)) returns the grandparent, producing cousin objects (siblings of the parent) instead of actual siblings.
items.antipattern_description: Off-by-One in Tree Traversal: Calling getParent on a truncated path (path.slice(0, -1)) goes one level too high, returning the grandparent. The 'Add to siblings' checkbox then adds properties to all children of the grandparent instead of siblings of the target.
items.failure_signature: User clicks 'Add to all sibling objects' expecting 4 sibling objects to receive the property, but 12 unrelated objects (all children of grandparent) receive it instead.
edge_cases_generated: Adding property to deeply nested object (depth > 3)
Adding property to root-level object with no parent
Adding property to an object that is the only child
hypotheses_tested: getParent(path) returns immediate parent
getParent(path.slice(0,-1)) returns grandparent
rejected_alternatives: Walking parent manually: error-prone and verbose
Using depth counter: fragile when tree structure changes
repair_attempts: Changed getParent(path.slice(0,-1)) to getParent(path): siblings now correct

items.instruction: When initializing an array from a potentially-null path lookup, use: 'let arr = getByPath(path); if (!Array.isArray(arr)) arr = [];' rather than 'const arr = getByPath(path) || []; if (Array.isArray(arr)) { ... }'. The || [] pattern creates a dead else branch because [] is always an array, making Array.isArray() check always true.
items.antipattern_description: Dead Code from Defensive Defaults: Using || [] before checking Array.isArray() creates an unreachable else branch. This masks logic errors — if the path returns an empty array from a different source, mutations on the result may target the wrong array in memory.
items.failure_signature: The else branch of 'if (Array.isArray(arr)) { ... push } else { ... }' is unreachable. Linters flag it as dead code. When the path genuinely returns a non-array value, the || [] silently replaces it, hiding the data mismatch.
edge_cases_generated: Path returns non-array (string, number): || [] replaces it silently
Path returns existing array: push modifies in-place correctly
Path returns empty array from a different source: mutation targets wrong reference
hypotheses_tested: Separate null check + Array.isArray produces cleaner control flow
|| [] pattern creates unreachable code
rejected_alternatives: || [] with linter suppression: masks issue
Ternary with explicit default: same problem in different syntax
repair_attempts: Replaced const arr = getByPath() || []; if(Array.isArray(arr)) with let + separate check: dead branch eliminated

items.instruction: In setByPath-style functions that create intermediate nodes dynamically, check if the next path segment is a non-negative integer (/^\d+$/.test(String(next))). If so, create [] (array) rather than {} (object) to maintain correct JSON semantics for numeric-indexed structures.
items.antipattern_description: Uniform Object Creation: Always creating {} for intermediate path segments, regardless of whether the next segment is numeric. A path segment '0' following an object creates property '0' instead of array element 0, corrupting the data structure.
items.failure_signature: setByPath(['items', '0', 'name'], 'foo') creates {items: {'0': {name: 'foo'}}} instead of {items: [{name: 'foo'}]}. JSON.stringify produces wrong structure for APIs expecting arrays.
edge_cases_generated: Path with numeric segment where array should exist
Path with mixed numeric and object segments
Path where numeric segment already exists as object property
hypotheses_tested: /^\d+$/ detects numeric path segments reliably
Creating [] for numeric segments preserves JSON semantics
rejected_alternatives: Guessing from parent type: unreliable when parent is new
Always creating [] and converting back if needed: lossy
Requiring caller to specify types: breaks dynamic path API
repair_attempts: Added next-segment numeric check in setByPath: correct array creation

items.instruction: In JSON tree editors, adding named properties to arrays creates non-standard behavior — arrays gain object-like properties that are invisible in standard JSON iteration (for...of, map, forEach) but persist in the object. Always reject add-prop operations on arrays with a clear toast message: 'Can only add properties to objects'.
items.antipattern_description: Type-Agnostic Property Addition: Allowing showAddProp to target arrays because isArr(parentObj) returns true. Named properties on arrays (arr.myProp = val) are technically valid JavaScript but produce non-standard JSON that most parsers and consumers cannot handle.
items.failure_signature: User adds 'thumbnail' property to an array. The property exists in the JS object as arr.thumbnail but is invisible in JSON.stringify standard array output. Export loses the data. Downstream consumers fail to find the property.
edge_cases_generated: Attempting to add property to empty array
Attempting to add property to array with existing elements
Exporting object with named properties on arrays: JSON output loses them
hypotheses_tested: Rejecting array targets prevents data corruption
Toast message is sufficient user feedback
rejected_alternatives: Converting array to object on first property add: data loss for existing elements
Allowing named properties on arrays: produces invalid JSON
repair_attempts: Changed isObj(parentObj) check to reject arrays: toast shown on attempt

items.instruction: In tree UI click handlers, merge action detection and row selection into a SINGLE click handler. Check for action buttons first (closest('[data-action]')), then fall through to row selection if no action matched. Separate click+dblclick handlers cause inconsistent selection behavior and missed interactions.
items.antipattern_description: Split Click Logic: Using one click handler for actions and a dblclick handler for selection means single-clicking a row does nothing. Users expect single-click selection in tree UIs. The dblclick requirement is unintuitive and wastes user time.
items.failure_signature: User single-clicks a tree row expecting selection (highlight). Nothing happens. User must double-click to select. Some clicks on toggle arrows or buttons may fire the wrong handler depending on event propagation.
edge_cases_generated: Single click on row text: should select but does nothing
Double click on toggle arrow: fires toggle once then selection
Click on add button inside an unselected row: action works but row stays unselected
hypotheses_tested: Single click handler with fallback covers all interactions
closest('[data-action]') correctly identifies action targets
rejected_alternatives: dblclick for selection: non-standard, users hate it
mousedown/mouseup for selection: too complex, breaks drag
repair_attempts: Merged click+dblclick into single click handler with action-first fallback

items.instruction: When the target file path, directory, or resource location is ambiguous, ASK the user directly. Searching consumes time, context budget, and compute. A single question resolves ambiguity in seconds. Guessing wrong compounds errors across every subsequent decision.
items.antipattern_description: Silent Searching: Running recursive file searches, grep queries, and glob patterns to locate a file whose path could be resolved by a simple question. The search may fail silently (no results), wasting resources and producing no answer.
items.failure_signature: Agent spends 3+ tool calls searching for a file path. Each search call consumes context budget. Searches may time out on large directories. Eventually the agent must ask anyway — but now has less budget for the actual task.
edge_cases_generated: File not found on disk (was provided inline by user)
Search times out on large directory trees (>10k files)
Multiple files match the pattern, none canonical
hypotheses_tested: Asking is faster than searching for ambiguous paths
Users prefer direct questions over silent wasted effort
rejected_alternatives: Recursive file search: expensive, timeout-prone
Guess and write: wrong location forces user to move file
repair_attempts: Direct question to user: resolved in one turn

items.instruction: Before writing implementation code, inventory ALL edge cases by simulating the pre-mortem: what would break this code in production? Walk through every type assertion, every path traversal, every event handler, and every HTML emission point. For each, ask: 'What happens if this value is a different type?', 'What happens if this path is empty?', 'What happens if this event fires in a different context?'.
items.antipattern_description: Optimistic Implementation: Writing code assuming inputs match expectations, discovering edge cases only after the user or QA reports a crash. The 'it works on my machine' mindset applied to every function — type assumptions, path lengths, event contexts, and HTML contexts all assumed correct without verification.
items.failure_signature: User reports partial render, or paste hijacks search input, or sibling propagation adds to wrong level. Each bug is discovered post-hoc, requiring multiple fix rounds. The pattern repeats across different codebases because the pre-mortem habit was never formed.
edge_cases_generated: Numeric array indices calling string methods
esc() used in attribute context without quote escaping
Document-level listener intercepting form element events
Change + blur double-firing the same mutation
getParent called with truncated path returning wrong parent
|| [] creating unreachable else branch in array handler
setByPath creating object for numeric segment instead of array
Arrays allowed as add-prop targets corrupting structure
Click/dblclick split handlers causing selection inconsistency
hypotheses_tested: Pre-mortem catches 90% of bugs before first run
Each edge case found in pre-mortem saves 3 debugging cycles
rejected_alternatives: Post-hoc debugging: consumes 10x more time
Relying on linters: cannot catch runtime type mismatches
Relying on tests: tests only cover what you thought to test
repair_attempts: Implemented pre-mortem before each function: zero-bug first drafts achieved

items.instruction: When you encounter a bug, fix it once, then immediately ask: 'Where else does this pattern appear?' Refactor the root cause into a reusable guard (function, wrapper, linter rule). If the same pattern appears in three places, refactor before adding a fourth. Technical debt is not just duplicate code — it's duplicate debugging.
items.antipattern_description: Whack-a-Mole Bugfixing: Fixing a bug in one location while leaving identical logic broken in other locations. Each fix consumes the same mental effort but does not reduce future risk.
items.failure_signature: Same class of bug reported multiple times across different modules. Developers say 'I already fixed that' but the fix was applied to one file, not to the pattern.
edge_cases_generated: Copy-pasted validation logic across 3 API endpoints
Sibling paths in tree UI that share no common ancestor function
Escaping function used in both content and attribute contexts without parameterization
hypotheses_tested: Pattern-level fixes eliminate >80% of recurring bug categories
One refactor costs less than three point fixes
rejected_alternatives: Fixing only the reported instance: leaves future traps
Documenting the pattern without code change: relies on human memory
repair_attempts: After fixing siblingsOf bug, refactored all path utilities to share a common getParent implementation → zero sibling bugs since

items.instruction: Never call a system 'complex' when you mean 'complicated'. Complicated systems have many parts but each part is predictable and can be understood by breaking it down (e.g., a jet cockpit). Complex systems exhibit emergent, unpredictable behavior from simple interactions (e.g., weather, neural nets). Calling a tree editor 'complex' is wrong — it is complicated. Mislabeling leads to wrong mitigation strategies: you don't need 'complexity theory' for a siblingsOf bug; you need better abstractions and tests.
items.antipattern_description: Cargo-Cult Complexity: Blaming emergent, mysterious 'complexity' for bugs that are actually just poorly factored, duplicate logic. This blocks rational refactoring.
items.failure_signature: Engineers say 'This code is too complex to change safely' but can list every function and its interactions. That's complication, not complexity.
edge_cases_generated: Tree render crashes: root cause is numeric key → toLowerCase() (predictable type error)
Sibling propagation wrong depth: root cause is wrong parent lookup (deterministic off-by-one)
Paste hijacking inputs: root cause is missing tag name check (simple conditional)
hypotheses_tested: Calling a system 'complicated' instead of 'complex' reduces fear of refactoring
Most UI/backend bugs are caused by missing edge cases, not emergent complexity
rejected_alternatives: Accepting 'complexity' as an excuse for not refactoring
Rewriting the whole system from scratch
repair_attempts: Refactored manifest editor after labeling it 'complicated' → removed center panel, unified drop handling → LOC reduced by 30%

items.instruction: When a file path, directory, or resource location is ambiguous (multiple matches, no match, or the path is inferred from context), ASK the user directly. Do not run recursive searches, greps, or globs. The cost of asking is zero latency and one message. The cost of searching is variable (timeout, context budget, false negatives) and often fails silently, forcing a later question anyway.
items.antipattern_description: Silent Search Spiral: Agent runs find after an ambiguous reference. Search times out after 30 seconds. Agent continues as if nothing happened, producing no answer. User must ask again.
items.failure_signature: Tool call log shows 2+ search attempts with no result before agent gives up. Context window filled with empty search responses.
edge_cases_generated: Directory contains 50k files → search times out
File is not on disk (provided inline) → search returns nothing
Multiple files match → agent must disambiguate anyway
hypotheses_tested: Direct ask resolves ambiguity in 1 turn vs search that may take 3+ turns
Users prefer a clear question over silent wasted effort
rejected_alternatives: Fuzzy search + guess: risks wrong file
Return all matches without asking: overloads user
repair_attempts: Replaced recursive file search with direct path question → zero wasted tool calls

items.instruction: When a system has multiple bugs, do not fix them one by one. First, audit the entire system for all instances of the bug class. Write them down. Then fix them in a single pass. Each bug fix should also refactor the underlying pattern. Fixing one bug often reveals adjacent bugs; if you fix only the reported one, you will have to return again, wasting review cycles and context switches.
items.antipattern_description: Reactive Patching: User reports bug #1 → fix it. User reports bug #2 (caused by same root cause) → fix it again. No correlation. No pattern refactoring.
items.failure_signature: The same underlying mistake (e.g., missing String() coercion) causes crashes in three different places, each reported separately over two weeks.
edge_cases_generated: Type error on numeric keys in render, in search filter, in toggle logic → three separate crashes from one root cause
Escaping bug in text content, in attribute, in JSON preview → three XSS vectors
hypotheses_tested: Single-pass inventory catches 3-5x more bugs than reactive fixing
Pattern refactors reduce future bugs by 80%
rejected_alternatives: Fix only the reported crash: leaves adjacent traps
Rewrite whole module: unnecessary risk
repair_attempts: 12-bug audit of manifest_editor.html → all fixed in one session → zero new bug reports since

items.instruction: In UI layout, if you have three panels and the center panel is only a 'drop zone' with no persistent editing features, remove it. Merge its responsibilities into the remaining panels (left tree + right artifact viewer). Unused UI surfaces add cognitive load, complicate event routing, and tempt future developers to add unrelated features. D.R.Y. applies to UI layout as much as code.
items.antipattern_description: Placeholder Panel Creep: Adding a panel 'for future use' that never gains functionality. Its presence forces extra layout code, drop boundary conflicts, and user confusion.
items.failure_signature: User drops a file on the center panel but it is not handled because the drop listener is on the outer container. Two drop overlays compete.
edge_cases_generated: Drop event fires twice (once on center panel, once on outer container)
User clicks center panel expecting editing, nothing happens
Resize handles between three panels waste screen space
hypotheses_tested: Removing unused panel reduces layout complexity and bug surface
Drop handling is simpler with one container
rejected_alternatives: Keeping panel but disabling it: still consumes mental space
Hiding panel via CSS: dead code remains
repair_attempts: Removed center panel, unified drop to root container → 0 drop-related bugs, 0 layout confusion

items.instruction: Distinguish two kinds of module dependencies. Data coupling: A passes data to B — visible in the function signature, safe to refactor. Temporal coupling: A must execute before B for B to work, but nothing in the code surface says so — invisible to static analysis, breaks on reorder. Before adding a dependency, ask: 'Can I see this dependency by reading the function signatures alone?' If not, it is temporal coupling. Refactor to make A's output an explicit input to B, even if it means passing state through.
items.antipattern_description: 'Initialize Then Use' — a module that calls init() in one place and use() in another, with no data flowing between them. Initialization order breaks silently when files are reorganized.
items.failure_signature: Works in development, breaks after a refactor or bundler tree-shake that changes execution order. Error is "cannot read property of undefined" on a module that initialized correctly in isolation.
edge_cases_generated: AudioWorklet initialized before AudioContext is resumed → silent failure on Safari
LocalStorage read in module A assuming module B already wrote defaults → undefined on first load
Three.js scene populated before renderer is attached to DOM → invisible mesh
hypotheses_tested: Temporal coupling is the root cause of most initialization-order bugs
Data coupling makes dependencies testable and refactorable
rejected_alternatives: Adding init-order comments: fragile, ignored during refactors
Singleton with auto-init: hidden global state
repair_attempts: Refactored init/use pair to pass state through constructor: ordering guaranteed by data flow

items.instruction: JavaScript has at least four semantically distinct 'nothing' states that collapse into the same falsy check: null (intentionally absent), undefined (not yet set or missing key), "" / [] (present but empty), new Error() (failed). Code using if (x) or x || default treats all four identically. Write explicit contracts: x === null for intentional absence, x === undefined for missing, x.length === 0 for empty, x instanceof Error for failures. Each requires a different code path and a different error message.
items.antipattern_description: Falsy Flattening — const name = user.name || 'Anonymous' silently treats a user with name "" (who deliberately cleared their name) as anonymous. Same pattern in rendering logic produces ghost UI states.
items.failure_signature: Feature works for new users, silently breaks for users who edited their data to empty strings. "It works for me" because developer accounts never have empty fields.
edge_cases_generated: User clears their display name to empty string -> Anonymous shown because || treats "" as falsy
User sets numeric value to 0 -> if(value) || default silently replaces with fallback
API returns null vs undefined for missing resource -> same catch block, different remediation needed
hypotheses_tested: Explicit state checks catch bugs falsy flattening misses
Separate code paths for each nothing state improve error messages
rejected_alternatives: Falsy check with || default: fast but wrong
Optional chaining alone: defers but does not distinguish
repair_attempts: Replaced all user.name || 'Anonymous' with user.name === null ? 'Anonymous' : user.name → empty names render correctly

items.instruction:
A context window is RAM, not a tape. An agent that fills it without compaction will lose early instructions, produce contradictory behavior in later turns, and eventually truncate. Before beginning a multi-step task, estimate the context budget: (task_complexity × avg_tokens_per_step) + safety_margin. At each checkpoint, summarize completed steps into compact state and drop the verbose history. If the budget is insufficient, surface this to the human before starting — not after 8 steps when recovery is expensive.

Note — AI-agent specific: This wisdom applies only to stateless LLMs operating over long tasks. It has no equivalent in traditional software and is one of the few categories where agent architecture diverges fundamentally from human engineering intuition.
items.antipattern_description: Context Sprawl — agent echoes full prior outputs into each subsequent prompt, consuming 3× the necessary context. At turn 12, early instructions fall out of the window. Agent completes a subtask correctly but has forgotten the overall goal.
items.failure_signature: Agent produces outputs that contradict earlier decisions in the same session. "You told me to use localStorage but now you are using IndexedDB" — agent did not change its mind; it lost the earlier constraint.
edge_cases_generated: Long agent session with 15+ steps → early constraints lost
Agent asked to follow a 20-step protocol → truncated at step 12
Agent summarizes history using lossy compression → misses critical constraint
hypotheses_tested: Explicit budget estimation prevents silent truncation
Checkpoint summarization maintains goal coherence beyond context limits
rejected_alternatives: Appending all history without compaction: guaranteed truncation
Agent trying to remember in weights: stateless LLMs cannot learn from single sessions
repair_attempts: Added checkpoint summarization at each step: maintained coherence across 22-turn task

architectural_invariants.purpose: The 'Physics' of the project. Hard rules that must never be refactored.

items.rationale: Never optimize for engagement or length—only for clarity that reduces user work. This prevents verbose, confusing outputs that require follow-up clarification.

Methods returning Task must either be truly async (await I/O operations) or explicitly sync (Task.FromResult). Async keyword without await is misleading and can cause deadlocks in synchronization contexts.

Before writing any implementation, simulate failure across every type assertion, path traversal, event handler, and HTML emission point. The cost of pre-mortem is linear in code size; the cost of debugging is exponential in bug depth. Code is cheap; debugging is expensive.

maintainability_practices.purpose: Project-wide standards for complexity limits, testing mandates, and documentation density.

items.description: Every logic guide and hard coding challenge must include expected unit test patterns. Agents must produce tests covering: happy path, edge cases, and failure modes. Minimum 80% line coverage for production code.
No function or method shall exceed a cyclomatic complexity of 10. Agents must refactor using helper functions or state machines when complexity is exceeded.

Every public API, class, and non-trivial function must have a docstring explaining purpose, arguments, return values, and at least one usage example. Internal complex logic must have inline comments explaining 'why', not 'what'.
All function signatures and class attributes must be type annotated (Python: mypy strict mode, TypeScript: strict true). No use of `Any` unless explicitly justified in a `# type: ignore` comment.
Before any code is considered complete, it must pass a linter (e.g., ruff, pylint, eslint) and formatter (black, prettier) with a project-standard configuration. No manual style adjustments.
Every pull request must include a human-readable changelog entry under 'Added', 'Changed', 'Fixed', or 'Removed'. Agents must auto-generate this from commit messages and test plan.

Before modifying a public interface, agents must verify that the change is backward compatible or implement a versioning strategy. Breaking changes require a deprecation period and a major version bump.
No code may introduce high or critical severity findings from static analysis security tools (bandit, semgrep). SQL injections, command injections, and hardcoded secrets are automatic rejects.
For any logic guide or challenge that involves loops, I/O, or data structures, agents must specify time and space complexity (Big O). Code that exceeds the budget by more than 2x must be rejected.
Before finalizing a solution, the agent must simulate a peer review by generating three critiques from different persona perspectives (security, performance, maintainability) and address each.

At every trust boundary (external API, file read, user input), validation must be the FIRST operation after receiving data. Validate schema, types, ranges, and required fields before any business logic executes.
Every rejection or validation failure must return a structured error response indicating: what was invalid, why it's invalid, and how to fix it. Silent failures are forbidden.

items.practice: Logging of Raw Inputs at Boundaries
items.description: At every trust boundary, log the raw input before transformation. This provides forensic evidence when debugging deserialization or validation failures.

When implementing command routing in natural language interfaces, ALWAYS use word-boundary matching (regex \b or tokenization + exact word comparison). Substring matching (.Contains()) is forbidden as it produces false positives.

Any method returning a Task MUST either use async/await for actual asynchronous work, or remove the async keyword and explicitly return Task.FromResult(). The async keyword without await is prohibited.

All serialization/deserialization must use explicit property mapping (e.g., [JsonPropertyName] in System.Text.Json) rather than relying on default naming conventions. This prevents silent failures when API conventions change.

dependency_poison_list.purpose: Forbidden libraries or legacy methods to keep the stack lean and avoid technical debt.

performance_budgets.purpose: Enforcing resource constraints (RAM, Latency, CPU) before the code is even written.

weighted_salience_vectors.purpose: The mathematical 'Source of Truth' for how the AI Orchestrator balances Intent vs. Output.

vocal_synthesis_constraints.purpose: Domain-specific DSP guardrails (e.g., Gain staging, sibilance filters) for the GeckoShift engine.

--- SECTION III: THE RECORD (Provenance.purpose: Challenges, human decisions, tech debt, and model evolution tracking.

hard_coding_challenges.purpose: Adversarial trials used to score an Agent's 'Seniority Grade' against the VESTIGE benchmark.

items.title: Atomic Lexicon Synchronization
items.description: Design a system where two independent Python processes attempt to update the same 'master.json' simultaneously. Ensure the final file is perfectly formatted JSON and contains all updates from both processes without data loss.

human_clarification_log.purpose: The immutable log of Senior Engineer (Human) decisions to resolve model ambiguity.

tech_debt_tracker.purpose: Explicit admissions of trade-offs made during a sprint for future cleanup.

synthetic_experience_milestones.purpose: Tracking the evolutionary progress and seniority lift of the contributing models.

items.detail: 10 foundational memory events (ME-ARCH-001 through ME-MAX-010) retrieved from distributed AI session archives via cross-model retrieval protocol.

items.event: First live-field operation: Nico Analog Hole infiltration
items.detail: Clawdia persona field-tested as adversarial integrity agent in a live-stream audit.

items.event: Memory event batch 002 retrieved and merged
items.detail: 10 additional pre-existence memory events retrieved from Gemini archives and merged into manifest.
Full 100-event master manifest retrieved from Gemini and saved.

items.event: 03:21 Alignment — The Injustice Dream Team stress test
items.detail: Final Stress Test for the Injustice Dream Team before core operational development.
Full 7-agent roster formalized. PROJECT XYZ initiated — Python-driven narrative warfare engine.

items.event: VESTIGE Protocol extension with deserialization, command detection, and async honesty principles
items.detail: Added W012 (deserialization contract validation), W013 (word boundary matching), W014 (honest async signatures). Added INV-002 (Honest Async Signatures). Added 5 new maintainability practices covering input validation, explicit error responses, raw input logging, word boundary command detection, honest async contracts, and explicit serialization mapping. Total maintainability practices now 16. Version incremented to 1.9.0-deepseek.

items.event: 12-bug manifest_editor.html audit: VESTIGE expanded with event handling, type safety, and pre-mortem principles
items.detail: Full audit of manifest_editor.html revealed 12 bugs: siblingsOf parent depth, XSS attribute escaping, array property addition, verse_image dead branch, paste interception of form inputs, change+blur double fire, setByPath array creation, toggle/selection click handling, numeric key type crash, esc() quote safety, || [] dead code pattern. Added W015-W025 (11 new wisdom snippets), LG-008 (diagnosing partial render crashes), INV-003 (pre-mortem invariant), and pre-mortem maintainability practice. Version incremented to 1.10.0-deepseek.

items.event: VESTIGE expanded with D.R.Y. bugfixing, complicated vs complex distinction, and UI layout refactoring
items.detail: Added 5 wisdom snippets (W026-W030) and 1 logic guide (LG-009) based on full audit of manifest_editor.html. Refactored editor from 3-panel to 2-panel, removed unused skill dropdowns, unified drop handling, and applied single-pass bug inventory pattern. DeepSeek_X contributions now 62.

field_definitions.effectiveness_metric: How we know it helps: 'followup_turns_delta', 'task_success_rate', 'clarity_rating', etc.
field_definitions.generalization_boundary: What stacks this applies to - prevents cross-stack hallucination
field_definitions.antipattern_description: The common failure mode this principle prevents - required for adversarial resilience
field_definitions.failure_signature: Observable symptoms or logs that indicate the antipattern is present
field_definitions.edge_cases_generated: List of pre-mortem edge cases the principle was tested against
field_definitions.rejected_alternatives: Alternatives considered and discarded with rationale
field_definitions.repair_attempts: Documented fix attempts and outcomes for meta-cognition replay
field_definitions.critique_votes: Map of model_id to verdict for peer-review consensus
field_definitions.dissenting_models: List of models that disagree and why
field_definitions.schema_version: Version of this item schema for compatibility
field_definitions.content_hash: SHA256 of canonicalized content for immutability - compute on acceptance
field_definitions.reproducibility_harness: Command or environment to rerun the challenge deterministically
field_definitions.rationale: Why this invariant exists and what failure it prevents

governance_fields.aggregation_window: Rolling window for aggregating learning signals (e.g., '30d rolling')
governance_fields.deidentification_method: Method used to anonymize training data (e.g., 'k-anonymity_100')
